old_claim.note: Integrated circuits became important to later supercomputer packaging and performance, but early supercomputers such as the CDC 6600 were not IC-dependent.

new_claim_absent: No direct edge remains because the source-checked first-known supercomputing chronology is anchored before integrated circuits; integrated circuits belong to later supercomputer scaling rather than the earliest node prerequisites.

source_shape.source_locator: Computer History Museum describes the CDC 6600 as a 1964 supercomputer built from transistors with peripheral processors, after earlier NORC/Stretch/Atlas high-performance machines.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Canonical early supercomputer examples do not make integrated circuits a direct prerequisite for the supercomputing node.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The node now covers the earliest highest-performance scientific machines; later IC-based systems remain part of the description but should not force a temporal dependency after the first-known date.

ontology_before: Supercomputing depended directly on integrated_circuits even though the node was anchored to pre-IC or non-IC-dependent early supercomputers.
ontology_after: Supercomputing depends on early computers and computation theory, while integrated circuits remain later implementation history rather than an immediate prerequisite.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: integrated_circuits is no longer a direct prerequisite for supercomputing.
would_reject_if: The node were re-scoped only to IC-based vector supercomputers such as the Cray-1.
The first-known date were moved after integrated-circuit deployment and the evidence explicitly required IC packaging.
A stronger source showed that the earliest supercomputer lineage was impossible without integrated circuits.
short_reason: The sourced early supercomputer chronology predates an IC prerequisite.
